coredata.dc:title: Nanoparticle platform comprising lipid-tailed pH-sensitive carbon dots with minimal drug loss
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Hongjae
Kim, Kyoung Sub
Na, Kun
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we synthesized a lipid-mimicking organic material (PCD_FA) that can surpass the efficacy of lipid-based nanoparticles and demonstrated its potential as a delivery vehicle for various hydrophilic drugs. PCD_FA is a conjugate of pH-sensitive carbon dots (PCDs) and fatty acids (FAs) and has potential applications in several fields owing to various combinations of carbon dots (CDs) and FAs. Similar to phospholipids, PCD-FAs have hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails that allow them to self-form nanoparticles (Coposomes) in the aqueous phase. Coposomes can easily combine various hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tail combinations, and several drugs can be encapsulated, or drug release patterns can be controlled according to each property. We analyzed the differences in size, drug loading efficiency, and drug release patterns of Coposomes depending on the type of FAs and characteristics of the encapsulated drugs. Additionally, cell entry and intracellular drug release mechanisms of the Coposomes were identified. The applicability of Coposomes as drug delivery carriers for tumor treatment has been demonstrated in comparison with that of liposomes formulation in tumor-bearing mouse models. Consequently, this study presents possibilities for the synthesis and application of various amphiphilic lipid-mimicking organic materials via the combination of CDs and FAs with various functions.

G) Cytotoxicity of PCD_OA, DOPC, DOPE, and DOTAP in L929 cells. (n = 6) * p < 0.05; ** p < 0.01; *** p < 0.001.

head.ce:title: Nanoparticle platform comprising lipid-tailed pH-sensitive carbon dots with minimal drug loss

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we synthesized a lipid-mimicking organic material (PCD_FA) that can surpass the efficacy of lipid-based nanoparticles and demonstrated its potential as a delivery vehicle for various hydrophilic drugs. PCD_FA is a conjugate of pH-sensitive carbon dots (PCDs) and fatty acids (FAs) and has potential applications in several fields owing to various combinations of carbon dots (CDs) and FAs. Similar to phospholipids, PCD-FAs have hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails that allow them to self-form nanoparticles (Coposomes) in the aqueous phase. Coposomes can easily combine various hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tail combinations, and several drugs can be encapsulated, or drug release patterns can be controlled according to each property. We analyzed the differences in size, drug loading efficiency, and drug release patterns of Coposomes depending on the type of FAs and characteristics of the encapsulated drugs. Additionally, cell entry and intracellular drug release mechanisms of the Coposomes were identified. The applicability of Coposomes as drug delivery carriers for tumor treatment has been demonstrated in comparison with that of liposomes formulation in tumor-bearing mouse models. Consequently, this study presents possibilities for the synthesis and application of various amphiphilic lipid-mimicking organic materials via the combination of CDs and FAs with various functions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, a successful case of nanomedicine for the COVID-19 vaccine demonstrated the in vivo mRNA efficacy of lipid-based nanoparticles (LNPs), and the applications of various LNPs are being extensively examined [1–6]. Research on LNPs, which began around 1960, developed applications that increased their use in 1995 when the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and European Medicines Agency (EMA) approved a liposomal formulation (Doxil®) as an anticancer agent [7,8]. LNP can produce various types of nanoparticles, such as liposomes, solid lipid nanoparticles, and lipid nanoparticles. Hence, they can efficiently encapsulate not only small molecules but also active pharmaceutical ingredients, such as proteins, peptides, and nucleic acids [9–11]. Accordingly, the efficacy of various LNP products (ONPATTRO®, Comirnaty®, and Spikevax®) has been proven. These products have been approved by the FDA and are currently used in clinical disease treatment [11–15]. However, although the excellent efficacy of LNPs has been demonstrated, studies examining the in vivo mechanism of action of LNPs are lacking. This is because the lipids available for LNP are limited with respect to chemical modification, and the physical properties are altered via compositional changes rather than through additional chemical modifications [3,16–19]. Therefore, the current LNP technology has limitations in controlling the release of encapsulated drugs and in vivo tracking, thereby limiting the analysis of LNP behavior in the human body and the study of efficacy mechanisms. Therefore, a new material with excellent biocompatibility that can easily be imparted additional functions via chemical modification and can control drug release and movement in the body, rendering the development of a drug delivery system that is superior to LNP possible.

Carbon dots (CDs) have excellent biocompatibility and are widely used in various fields, such as sensing, photodynamic therapy (PDT), and photothermal therapy (PTT) [20–29]. In addition, they have been classified as promising nanomaterials that can be applied in bioimaging because of their excellent light-gathering properties and high solubility [23]. CDs have the advantage of being able to facilitate the design of surfaces with various functional groups, such as carboxy, amine, and hydroxy groups, depending on the precursors used for synthesis [21–23]. Furthermore, it is a nanomaterial that can be easily and cheaply synthesized by microwave or hydrothermal methods using various renewable carbon sources (cellulose [30], tea [31], fruits [32], and even roast beef [33]). However, CDs have limitations with respect to the encapsulation of various medicines owing to their excellent hydrophilicity, rendering their use as drug delivery systems without additional functions difficult.

In this study, we describe the fabrication of fatty acid (FAs)-conjugated pH-sensitive CDs (PCD_FAs), nanoparticles with PCD_FAs containing various hydrophilic small molecules, and their feasibility as drug delivery carriers for disease treatment. PCDs were synthesized in a microwave reactor using citric acid and 1-(3-aminopropyl)imidazole as precursors and had various reactive groups such as carboxy, amine, and hydroxy groups on their surfaces [34–38]. As in Scheme 1 , PCD_FAs are synthesized by covalent bonding of PCDs with oleic acid (OA), palmitic acid (PA), and stearic acid (SA). PCD_FAs have a hydrophilic PCDs head and hydrophobic FAs tail; therefore, nanoparticles are formed in the aqueous phase, and we define these nanoparticles as Coposomes. The types of drugs that can be encapsulated via the use of Coposomes have been demonstrated by encapsulation of hydrophilic drugs with various surface charges, such as the neutral charge of doxorubicin (Dox), anionic charge of gemcitabine (Gem), and cationic charge of methylene blue (MB). The effects of different lipid tail lengths and types coupled with the PCDs on the size, drug loading efficiency, and drug release rate of the Coposomes were evaluated in comparison with those observed using conventional liposomes. The cell entry and drug release mechanisms of the Coposomes were analyzed in human pancreatic cancer cells. In vivo, real-time localization monitoring capacity without the addition of a specific fluorescent dye and the tumor growth inhibitory efficacy of Coposomes were evaluated in a Panc-1 tumor-bearing Balb/c nude mouse model. The PCD-FAs described in this study can be synthesized via facile chemical synthesis, and numerous combinations can be obtained by combining various FAs and CDs. In other words, it is a new platform technology that can be applied to various fields and not just to overcome the limitations of drug delivery technology using existing LNPs.

First, 1 g of citric acid (CA, Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA) was dissolved in 15 mL of distilled water (D·W) in a flask, and different amounts of 4 M 1-(3-aminopropyl) imidazole (API; Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA) were added with vigorous stirring. The solution was then placed in a 35 mL vial, which was heated in microwave synthesizer (Discover 2.0, CEM Corporation, NC, USA) at 300 W, 250 °C for 5 min. After cooling to room temperature (RT), the yellow-brown liquid obtained was dissolved in D·W. Then, it was dialyzed against D·W using a dialysis membrane (MWCO:100–500, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) for 2 days (exchanged three times per day) to eliminate the remaining free CA and API, followed by lyophilization for use in further experiments. To characterize PCDs, UV–vis absorption, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-alpha, Thermo, U.K.), and high-resolution mass spectrometry (HR-MS) were performed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2350, Shimadzu, Japan), and JMS 700 (Jeol, Japan), respectively.

The conjugation of PCDs and FAs was performed via conventional N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC; Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS; Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA,) coupling esterification. Briefly, 100 mg of OA, 80 mg of PA, and 100 mg of SA (Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA) were dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO; Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA; 10 mL) and DCC (94.8 mg, 460 μM_1.3 fold FA in moles), NHS (52.9 mg, 460 μM_1.3 fold FA in moles) and activated OA, PA, and SA in 6 h at RT. After activation, dicyclohexyl urea (DCU) was removed using a 0.45 μm Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) syringe filter and added to PCDs (180 mg) each vial that was dissolved in DMSO. After 24 h of reaction, the solution was purified by precipitation in diethyl ether (200 mL, 3 times), and LH-20 hydrophobic chromatographic columns (mobile phase: pure methanol) were used. Then, the final solution was dried in a vacuo to remove methanol. PCD_FA synthesis was evaluated using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2350, Shimadzu, Japan). Next, we compared the fluorescence of the PCDs at the same concentration as those of the synthesized PCD_OA, PCD_PA, and PCD_SA.

The CMC of PCDs and PCD_OA at different molar ratios in water was measured at RT using a microplate reader (Bio-Tek, VT, USA) with 1.4 μM Hoechst 33342 (used as a fluorescent probe) [6]. First, CD and PCD_OA were dissolved in water at various concentrations, and Hoechst 33342 stock solution was added to a 1:1 (v/v) solution in a 5 mL glass vial. The cells were incubated for 1 h in a dark room for sufficient interaction, with vortexing every 5 min. After incubation, each solution was transferred to a clear-bottom black 96-well plate to measure the fluorescence of Hoechst 33342 (ex: 350 nm, em: 481 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acid-base titrations for measuring the pKa of PCD_OA
ce:section.ce:para: Prepared by dissolving PCD_OA to 1 mg/mL in D·W and adjusting the pH to 3.0, by adding HCl. Subsequently, 20 μL of 1 M NaOH was added to check the pH change. The equivalence point was reached at the titration point when enough base was added to the acid so that the concentration of [H+] in the solution was equal to the concentration of [OH-]. This point was calculated using GraphPad Prism software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation and characterization of drug-loaded Coposomes (Drug@Copo_FAs)
ce:section.ce:para:
Coposomes and liposomes were prepared using a thin-film hydration method. PCD_FA (12 mg) and cholesterol (Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA; 0.2 mg) for Coposomes or 1,2-dioleoyl-sn-glycero−3-phosphocholine (DOPC, Avanti Polar Lipids, Huntsville, AL, USA, 10 mg) and cholesterol (0.5 mg) were dissolved in ethanol (5 mL). The ethanol was evaporated using a rotary evaporator (50 °C, 160 rpm). After ethanol was completely removed, the thin film was hydrated using a 1 mg/mL drug solution (Gem, MB, Dox, Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA), and the Coposomes and liposomes were sonicated using a tip sonicator (VCX-750, Sonics & Materials. Inc. Newtown, USA) for 5 min. Next, the Drug@Copo_FAs and drug-loaded liposomes (Drug@Lipo) were centrifuged at 3500 rpm using a filtration device (Millipore Corp., Billerica, MA, USA) with a 10 kDa filter membrane to remove unloaded drugs. The size, zeta potential, and morphology of Drug@Copo_FA and Drug@Lipo were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS; Zetasizer Nano Zs, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). Gem@Copo_OA was observed using a field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM; JEM-F200(TFEG), JEOL Ltd., Japan) after staining with a 2% solution of uranyl acetate to obtain external images with enhanced contrast. To investigate the drug-loading efficiency (LE) and loading content (LC) in the Drug@Copo_FAs suspension, the amount of Gem at Gem@Copo_FAs was quantified after dissolution using a 5% Triton X-100 solution by high-performance liquid chromatography ultraviolet (HPLC-UV, Shimadzu, Japan) system at 275 nm (Thermo Scientific Hypersil C-18 column mobile phase: acetonitrile (ACN): water = 4:6; flow rate:1 mL/min; injection volume: 10 μL). The amounts of MB and Dox were quantified after dissolving in a 5% Triton X-100 solution using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The LE and LC of the various complexes were calculated using the following equations:
(1)
LE
%
=
drug loaded / drug input
× 100
(2)
LC
%
=
drug loaded / Coposomes total
× 100

To evaluate the drug release pattern from Drug@Copo_FAs, Drug@Copo_FA and Drug@Lipo suspensions (1 mL) were placed into dialysis membranes (MWCO 3.5 kDa, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA), which were then placed into dialysis tubes filled with 7 mL of pH 7.4 phosphate buffer saline (PBS; Welgene, Daegu, Korea) solution (n = 3). The dialysis tubes were incubated in a shaking water bath (50 rpm) in the dark at 37 °C. At predetermined intervals (1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, and 24 h), all outer solutions were removed and exchanged with fresh PBS buffer. Subsequently, the amount of Gem released in the PBS solution was measured using an HPLC-UV system. The HPLC-UV analysis was performed at RT using a Thermo Scientific Hypersil C-18 column. HPLC-grade ACN and water as the mobile phase (4:6) were delivered at a flow rate of 1 mL/min (injection volume, 10 μL). The gem was detected at 275 nm. The amounts of MB and Dox in PBS were quantified using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. MB and Dox were detected at 660 nm and 480 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the release patterns of Gem@Copo_OA and Gem@Lipo in plasma were compared in human plasma (Korean Red Cross, Korea) according to the aforementioned Gem release kinetics method.

Panc-1 (human pancreatic cancer) cells were obtained from the Korean Cell Line Bank. Cells were cultured in DMEM-high glucose medium (Gibco BRL, Invitrogen Corp., CA, USA) with 1% antibiotic solution (Gibco BRL, Invitrogen Corp., CA, USA) and 10% heat-inactivated FBS (Gibco BRL, Invitrogen Corp., CA, USA) at 37 °C (100% humidity and 5% CO2). The cells were subcultured for 2–3 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of cytotoxicity with conventional phospholipids
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity was assessed using the MTT colorimetric assay. L929 cells were seeded into 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells per well), and 24 h after seeding, PCD_OA, 1,2-dioleoyl-3-trimethylammonium-propane (DOTAP), DOPC, and 1,2-dioleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine (DOPE) (all phospholipids were purchased from Avanti Polar Lipids, Huntsville, AL) were added to each well at various concentrations (0.1 mL). The wells were returned to the incubator for 12 h. After incubation, the plate was rinsed twice with Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS, Gibco BRL, Invitrogen Corp., CA, USA) to remove samples that were not internalized into the cells. The cells were then incubated for an additional 52 h. The plate was then rinsed twice with DPBS and an appropriate concentration of the MTT reagent (Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA) for 4 h. The resulting formazan crystals were dissolved in DMSO. Absorbance intensity was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (Bio-Tek, VT, USA).

The effect of Coposomes on cell viability was assessed using the MTT colorimetric assay. Panc-1 cells were seeded into 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells per well), and 24 h after seeding, free Gem, Copo_OA, Gem@Copo, and Gem@Lipo (serial dilution started at 10 μM of Gem) were added to each well at various concentrations (0.1 mL volume). The wells were returned to the incubator for 4 h. The subsequent process was performed using a previously described protocol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confirmation of the cellular uptake and endocytosis pathway
ce:section.ce:para: To observe the cellular uptake and localization of rhodamine-123-loaded Coposomes (Rho@Copo_OA), Panc-1 cells were cultured in six-well plates (1 × 105 cells per well) and incubated for 24 h in a cell incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). After 24 h, the medium was replaced with serum-free (SF) medium containing rhodamine-123 or Rho@Copo_OA, and the treated cells were incubated for predetermined intervals at pH 7.4. The cells were then washed thrice with DPBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. The cells were observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM 710 Meta, Carl Zeiss, Germany) and a flow cytometry cell sorter (FACS) with a Becton-Dickinson FACS Canto II (San Jose, CA, USA). Fluorescence images were analyzed using the CLSM image software (Zen series, Carl Zeiss, Germany). For endocytosis inhibition experiments, the cells were preincubated for one hour at 37 °C with different endocytic inhibitors following concentrations at pH 7.4 and pH 6.4 : 5-(N-ethyl-N-isopropyl) amiloride (EIPA, 50 μM), wortmannin (100 nM), chlorpromazine (Pitstop, 10 μM), and nystatin (50 μM). All chemical inhibitors were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. St. Louis, MO, USA. One hour later, the inhibitor was removed, and Gem@Copo_OA was added at a concentration of approximately 1.5 times the IC50 value in SF media for 4 h. After Gem@Copo_OA treatment, the medium was replaced with a complete medium. The MTT assay was performed according to the aforementioned protocol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confirmation of the endosomal escape ability
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the endosomal escape ability of the Coposomes, endosome-mimicking liposomes were fabricated. 1,2-dioleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phospho-l-serine (DOPS), DOPC, DOPE, 1,2-dioleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-(7-nitro-2-1,3-benzoxadiazol-4-yl) (NBD-PE), and 1,2-dioleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-(lissamine rhodamine B sulfonyl) (Rho-PE) (all phospholipids were purchased from Avanti Polar Lipids, Huntsville, AL, USA) were mixed in a molar ratio of 25:25:48:1:1 to form a thin film by rotary evaporation. Then, the solutions were hydrated with PBS at pH 7.4 or pH 6.4 and homogenized using a tip sonicator. After fabrication, pH 7.4 or pH 6.4 PBS was added to black 96-well plates (100 μL per well), and 5 μL of endosomal-mimicking liposomes (1.0 mM) was added to each well. A total of 20 μL of Coposomes was added to the wells and incubated at 37 °C for 120 min. Fluorescence intensity was measured using a microplate reader. (Ex/Em = 465 nm/520 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution analysis and tumor infiltration imaging
ce:section.ce:para: All procedures were approved (approval No. CUK-IACUC-2022-012) by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of the College of Pharmacy, the Catholic University of Korea (Republic of Korea) and Use Committee in accordance with the “Principles of Laboratory Animal Care,” NIH publication no.85–23, revised in 1985. To create a pancreatic cancer model, male BALB/c nude mice were subcutaneously inoculated with Panc-1 cells (1 × 107 cells/mouse). Panc-1 tumor-bearing nude mice were randomly divided into three groups (n = 4). Treatments were administered when the tumor size reached approximately 50–100 mm3. Solutions of Rho@Copo_OA and Rho@Lipo were injected into Panc-1 tumor-bearing mice via the tail vein. Fluorescence was obtained using the FOBI imaging system (Fluorescence-labeled Organism BioImaging, NEO Science, Suwon, Korea) at different time points for up to 12 h. After 24 h, the mice were euthanized, and the major organs and tumors were excised and analyzed using FOBI. The fluorescence signal intensity was quantified using the NEO image software. After two days, the mice were euthanized, and tumors were fixed for 24 h in 4% paraformaldehyde. After fixation, tumors were frozen, sectioned into slices with a thickness of 10 μm, and observed using CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo tumor growth inhibition test
ce:section.ce:para: Solutions of PBS, free Gem, and Gem@Copo_OA were injected into Panc-1 tumor-bearing BALB/c nude mice via the tail vein (dose: 5 mg/kg Gem). The tumor size and body weight were measured at each time point. Tumor volume was calculated using the following equation: tumor volume = (length×width2)/2.

To investigate histology, PBS-, free Gem-, Gem@Lipo-, and Gem@Copo_OA-treated Panc-1 tumor-bearing nude mice were euthanized, and major organs were collected and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 24 h. After paraffin removal, the tissue sections were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and observed using a slide scanner (APERIO CS2, Leica, Germany). Hepatotoxicity and renal toxicity were evaluated by measuring alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), total bilirubin (T-Bili), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), and creatinine (Crea) levels in serum isolated from blood samples collected two weeks after IV injection.

Results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The results derived from the in vitro, ex vivo, and in vivo studies are expressed as mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM). Asterisks indicate statistical significance determined by one-way and two-way ANOVA with Sidak's multiple comparison test.

In this study, we prepared PCDs with pH-dependent surface charge conversion using CA and API as precursors. PCDs were synthesized using a microwave synthesis method and purified by dialysis against distilled D·W The structures of the synthesized PCDs were confirmed by FT-IR and XPS analyses. The PCDs demonstrated the -OH group of citric acid and the -NH group of the imidazole ring, along with the CC peak in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S1A). XPS analysis further confirmed the presence of the NH structure in the imidazole ring of the PCDs (Fig. S1B,C). According to DLS measurements and FE-TEM images, the PCDs showed a hydrodynamic size of 4–5 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. S1D, Fig. S2). The PCDs exhibited yellow fluorescence under UV irradiation (Fig. S1E, Fig. S3). The surface charge of the PCDs changed from anionic to cationic as the pH changed from 8.4 to 5.5, which was the result of the ionization of the imidazole in the API (Fig. S1F). These results indicate that the PCDs used in this study could emit fluorescence signals and had hydroxy and amine groups on the surface; therefore, an additional chemical modification was facile, and the surface charge was converted according to pH.

To prepare a material that could replace lipids used in various LNPs, PCD-FA conjugates were developed by covalently bonding the carboxy group of FAs to the amine group of the PCDs, as shown in Fig. 1A. In this study, to analyze the differences in drug loading efficiency and drug release rate according to the characteristics of the FA chain conjugated to PCDs, we synthesized PCD_FAs conjugates with three types of FAs: C-18 OA (PCD_OA), C-16 saturated PA (PCD_PA), and C-18 saturated SA (PCD_SA). PA and SA were used to compare the characteristics of PCD_FAs based on the number of carbon atoms at FAs (length of FA chain) or the presence of double bonds, to compare with OA, an unsaturated FA, and to confirm their applicability to general FAs. The successful synthesis of PCD_FA conjugates was confirmed by changes in the absorbance shift and particle size. The UV absorption peak of PCDs shifted from 230 nm to 236 nm (PCD_OA), 237 nm (PCD_PA), and 243 nm (PCD_SA) (Fig. 1B). Additionally, the successful synthesis of PCD_FAs significantly increased the hydrodynamic size of PCD_FA compared to that of PCDs at the same concentration (0.5 mg/mL) (Fig. 1C). The degree of substitution of OA bound to PCDs was calculated using HR-MS, and PCD_OA showed that the molecular weight of the PCDs increased from 500 to 800 Da to 1000–1100 Da, indicating that one to two molecules of OA were bound per PCD (Fig. S4.) To determine how the binding of FAs to PCDs altered the characteristics of existing PCDs, we performed a comparative analysis of PCD_OA and PCDs. The CMC of PCD was <1 mg/mL due to the hydrophobic nature of the imidazole ring included in the precursor API. In comparison, PCD_OA showed a notable decrease in CMC due to increased hydrophobicity compared to that of the PCDs. (Fig. 1D). The unique properties of the PCDs, such as pKa and fluorescence, were maintained without significant differences (Fig. 1E,F). The pKa of PCD_OA was approximately 6.6, which could represent the cationic charge in the tumor microenvironment and early endosomes. This may promote cell infiltration and endosomal escape by nanoparticles. PCD_OA showed similar or reduced cytotoxicity compared to lipids, such as DOTAP, DOPC, and DOPE, which are commonly used in LNP formulations (Fig. 1G). These results indicate that PCD_FAs emit fluorescence, change their surface charge at approximately pH 6.6, and form nano-sized particles on their own owing to their amphiphilic property. They can also be used as suitable candidates for new drug delivery systems with fewer cytotoxicity concerns.

The purpose of this study was to determine whether the synthesized PCD_FAs could be used as drug delivery platforms. Nanoparticles encapsulating various drugs were fabricated using three FAs (PA, SA, and OA) and three hydrophilic drugs (Gem, MB, and Dox), and their properties were compared and analyzed. Here, we refer to nanoparticles based on PCD_FAs as Coposomes. When fabricating the Coposomes, the hydrophilic region (PCD head) is expected to face the water. In contrast, the hydrophobic region of the FA undergoes hydrophobic interactions, resulting in the aggregation of PCD molecules and the formation of spherical particles. To differentiate between various drugs and FAs, the enclosed drugs and FAs were named separately in Drug@Copo_FAs format. To manufacture drug delivery carriers using PCD_FAs, nanoparticles were fabricated by thin-film hydration of PCD_OA, PCD_PA, and PCD_SA with cholesterol (Fig. 2A). Liposomes were prepared in the same way as the Coposomes using DOPC and cholesterol, and the efficacy of each was evaluated and compared. The size, surface charge, polydispersity index (PDI), drug loading efficiency, and drug loading content of liposomes and Coposomes are presented in Table 1 . The successful encapsulation of hydrophilic drugs suggests that Coposomes are nanoparticles with a hydrophilic core. Drug@Copo_FAs could be manufactured with sizes ranging from 120 nm to 160 nm depending on the unsaturated or saturated lipid tail conjugated to the PCDs. For hydrophilic drugs, the size of Drug@Copo_FAs was not affected, even if the surface charge of the encapsulated drugs was different. The drug encapsulation efficiency of Drug@Copo_FAs was affected by the surface charge of the encapsulated drug, which showed various encapsulation efficiencies ranging from 30 to 84%. Additionally, different Gem encapsulation efficiencies ranging from 60 to 84% were observed depending on the lipid tail conjugated to the PCDs. Cholesterol in the Coposome played a strategic role as a stabilizer; steroids could increase the packing of the hydrophobic tail in PCD_OA and improve vesicle resistance to aggregation (Fig. S5). In this study, Gem@Copo_OA, which showed the most appropriate size and highest drug encapsulation efficiency, was selected and evaluated for its effectiveness as a drug delivery carrier for tumor treatment. To elucidate the structure of Coposomes, we evaluated the encapsulation of hydrophilic drugs using PCD_FAs and cholesterol. FE-TEM images and hydrophilic drug encapsulation confirmed that Gem@Copo_OA was spherical with a hydrophilic inner core. In addition, the membrane thickness (about 20 nm) of the PCD_OA bilayer was confirmed. (Fig. 2B and Fig. S6). Gem@Copo_OA remained constant in size, even when the pH was changed from 7.4 to 6.4 (Fig. 2C). In contrast, the surface charge of Gem@Copo_OA was converted from anionic (−55 mV at pH 7.4) to cationic (45 mV at pH 6.4) when the negative pH was changed from 7.4 to 6.4 (Fig. 2D). The changes in surface charge could be attributed to the characteristics of the imidazole ring incorporated during the production of PCD. The pKa of the imidazole ring is approximately 6.9, which means that at pH 7.4, it is protonated and carries a negative charge. On the other hand, at pH 6.4, it is deprotonation and carries a positive charge. Therefore, the difference in pH can lead to a shift in the charge state of the imidazole ring, resulting in changes in the overall surface charge of the Coposomes. Furthermore, Gem@Copo_OA still emitted fluorescence (Fig. 2E). These results indicate that the unique properties of existing PCD, such as pH-responsive charge conversion and fluorescence emission, were well maintained even in the form of Coposomes. Interestingly, Gem@Copo_OA exhibited a reduced drug release rate of <10% over 24 h in PBS (pH 7.4) and plasma solutions, showing a remarkable decrease in the initial drug release amount compared to that of Gem@Lipo (Fig. 2F,G). The drug release rates of Gem@Copo_PA and Gem@Copo_SA, which used other lipid tails, showed a similar trend as that of Gem@Copo_OA. Although the notably lower drug release amount of MB@Copo_OA and Dox@Copo_OA could increase by up to 20% over 24 h because of the properties of the encapsulated drug, it was still significantly reduced compared to the drug release amount of MB@Lipo or Dox@Lipo (Fig. 2H,I). The pattern of remarkably lower initial drug release of Drug@Copo_FAs suggests its potential as a drug delivery vehicle that could maximize drug efficacy and minimize side effects by minimizing the non-specific release of encapsulated drugs. Although the exact mechanism needs to be analyzed further, it was assumed that the significantly reduced drug release of Drug@Copo_FAs owing to the stability of Coposomes was attributed to the interaction between FA and cholesterol as well as between the imidazole groups present in PCD. Based on the above experiments, Coposomes can be referred to as a platform technology that can be used to obtain various combinations of numerous FAs and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity, cellular entry, and drug release mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To determine how the significantly reduced initial drug release pattern of Drug@Copo_FAs affected cytotoxicity, it was evaluated in human pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma Panc-1 cells. To confirm the cytotoxic effect of Gem@Copo, Copo_OA (empty Coposome with PCD_OA), free Gem, and Gem@Lipo were used as control groups. Cells were incubated in a serum-free medium with Copo_OA, free Gem, Gem@Lipo, or Gem@Copo_OA for 4 h, followed by further incubation for an additional 52 h in the dark. When assessed at various blank Coposome doses, no significant cytotoxicity was observed, even at the highest concentration (Fig. 3A). The IC50 values of free Gem, Gem@Lipo, and Gem@Copo_OA in Panc-1 cells were approximately 0.72 μM, 0.65 μM, and 0.32 μM, respectively. This result indicates that cytotoxicity was improved with nanoformulation compared to that with free Gem, and Gem@Copo_OA exhibited superior cytotoxicity compared to Gem@Lipo. The Drug@Copo_FAs evaluated in this study were a novel type of nanoparticle encapsulated with a fluorescent dye, Rho, that can confirm the intracellular release behavior of hydrophilic drugs. Rho@Lipo and Rho@Copo_OA were prepared in the same way to identify the mechanism of entry into cells and drug release. The intracellular fluorescence intensity of Rho and PCDs gradually increased for up to 4 h as the cell exposure time increased (Fig. 3B,C). In the same period, Rho@Copo_OA showed significantly increased Rho cell uptake compared to Rho@Lipo (Fig. 3D). The amount of Rho present in the cell indicates that the cargo molecules of Coposomes were effectively released into the cytosol. Therefore, we conducted several experiments to confirm the mechanism by which Coposomes were ingested by the cells and their endosomal escape ability. The two primary routes for the entry of nanoparticles into cells involve fusion and endocytosis (Fig. S7). To identify the cell entry pathway of the Coposomes, Panc-1 cells were treated with inhibitors of each pathway of fusion and endocytosis, and differences in the cytotoxicity of Gem@Copo_OA were evaluated. An incubation temperature of 4 °C inhibited energy-consuming endocytosis, allowing particles to enter the cell only via fusion. 5-[N-ethyl-N-isopropyl] amiloride (EIPA) [39], wortmannin [40], pitstop [41], and nystatin [42] have been used as inhibitors of macropinocytosis, phagocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, and caveolin-mediated endocytosis, respectively. These inhibitor-pre-treated Panc-1 cells were treated with Gem@Copo_OA (Gem concentration:1.5 times the IC50 value), and the MTT assay was performed. Coposomes are believed to use different entry pathways under conditions involving pH 7.4 and 6.4. At pH 7.4, the cytotoxicity of Coposomes was significantly reduced at 4 °C in EIPA-treated cells, suggesting that Coposomes entered the cell via the micropinocytosis pathway (Fig. 3E and Fig. S8). However, at pH 6.4, the cytotoxicity was reduced by 60%, even at 4 °C. Hence, the surface charge of the Coposomes was converted to a cationic charge, and the molecules entered the cell via diffusion as well as micropinocytosis pathways. We hypothesized that Rho@Copo_OA had a higher amount of intracellular Rho than Rho@Lipo, which could be attributed to the more potent endosomal escape ability of Coposomes to escape than Liposome. To prove this, we fabricated liposomes mimicking endosomes (LME) [43,44], and the amount of fusion with liposomes or Coposomes was compared according to pH. The interaction of liposome with LME was maintained constant with pH changes, whereas the Coposomes showed higher fusion with the LME at pH 6.4 of the early endosome (Fig. 3F). Therefore, Coposomes, rather than liposomes, could fuse with the endosomal membrane according to the changing endosome pH, releasing internal cargo molecules into the cytosol. Based on the results, an experiment was conducted to confirm the behavior of the Coposomes when they were ingested by cells using confocal microscopy (Fig. 3G). The intracellular uptake of the Coposomes could also be detected using the fluorescence of the PCD itself. Panc-1 cells were treated with Rho@Copo, and the localization of Rho and Coposome fluorescent signals was observed using confocal microscopy. After incubating Rho@Copo in Panc-1 cells for 4 h, the cytoplasmic localization of Rho and Coposome fluorescence signals were analyzed. The intensity of each fluorescence signal was different at the same location, indicating that Rho was properly released from Coposomes (Fig. 3H, I). These results suggest that Gem@Copo_OA minimizes drug release in the extracellular environment while boosting drug release inside the cells. As the method by which Gem@Copo_OA could release Gem from inside the cell was highly likely to involve Coposomes and cell membrane diffusion, the entry mechanism of Gem@Copo_OA into the cell was further elucidated. These results indicate that Gem@Copo_OA increases the amount of drug entering cells by reducing the initial drug release and promoting drug release by membrane diffusion inside the cells, resulting in better cancer cytotoxicity than liposome.

To evaluate whether the ability of Coposomes to control drug release affects the efficacy of the encapsulated drug reaching the tumor site, Rho@Lipo and Rho@Copo_OA were intravenously injected into a Panc-1 tumor-bearing Balb/c nude mouse model. Rho@Copo_OA was observed at the tumor site 2 h after injection and was gradually accumulated at the tumor site for at least 12 h (Fig. 4A). Rho@Copo_OA not showed a faster accumulation of higher amounts of Rho at the tumor site than Rho@Lipo but also remained at the tumor site for a longer time (at least 48 h) after injection (Fig. 4B-D). Generally, intravenously injected nanoparticles can accumulate at the tumor site owing to the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, but the release of the drug before reaching the tumor site directly leads to drug loss [45–47]. Thus, it was hypothesized that the superior ability of Gem@Copo_OA to accumulate at the tumor site was attributed to its ability to reduce the initial drug release, thereby minimizing drug loss. Notably, Coposomes composed of PCD could serve as fluorescent probes; therefore, the distribution of the drug and carrier in the body could be confirmed simultaneously by tracing the distribution of the drug and that of Coposomes after injection into the body (Fig. 4E). These properties of Coposomes could also help analyze and interpret the distribution of drug delivery systems in specific tissues (Fig. 4F). These results suggest that Coposomes could improve the efficacy of existing drug delivery systems by enabling tracking of in vivo distribution, as well as exerting an improved tumor-specific accumulation effect than that of liposomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo tumor growth inhibition test and toxicity evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the tumor growth-inhibitory efficacy of Gem@Copo_OA in an animal tumor model, a tumor model was established using Panc-1 cells, a pancreatic cancer cell line. Gem@Copo_OA exhibited superior cytotoxic efficacy compared to the free Gem and Gem@Lipo groups, without any body weight loss (Fig. 5A,B). When the tumor growth tendency of each individual group was analyzed, the tumor growth was slightly suppressed in the free Gem-and Gem@Lipo-treated mice, whereas the tumor size continuously increased in the PBS-treated mice (Fig. 5C-E, Fig. S9). In contrast, mice treated with Gem@Copo_OA showed an overall reduction in tumor size (Fig. 5F). Tumor tissues were harvested 14 days after each sample treatment and subjected to histological analysis. The most extensive cell death was observed in Gem@Copo_OA-treated mice (Fig. 5G). These results suggest that Gem@Copo_OA could be used as an effective drug delivery system owing to not only increased efficacy of tumor site accumulation but also adequate drug release from the tumor site and excellent tumor growth inhibitory effect. Although Gem@Copo_OA had a therapeutic effect on pancreatic cancer, it did not demonstrate adverse effects on major organs (off-target effects). Therefore, the major organs (the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and blood were collected from Panc-1 tumor-bearing BALB/c mice 14 days after the injection of Free Gem, Gem@Lipo, and Gem@Copo_OA. No significant differences were observed in the concentrations of ALT, AST, T-bili, Crea, and BUN, which are specific markers of liver function, in Free Gem, Gem@Lipo, and Gem@Copo_OA-treated mice compared to those in PBS-treated mice (Fig. 5H-L). Additionally, no toxicity toward major organs, such as the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney, was observed in the H&E staining images of Gem@Copo_OA-treated mice (Fig. S10). These results suggest that Gem@Copo_OA can improve tumor therapeutic efficacy while minimizing adverse effects.

In summary, we demonstrated a new type of nanoparticle, Drug@Copo_FAs, which can encapsulate various hydrophilic drugs (Gem, MB, and Dox) via the development of PCD conjugates with various FAs (OA, PA, and SA). Drug@Copo_FAs is a platform technology that forms spherical nanoparticles with a size of 110 to 160 nm, which can be combined in various ways depending on the FAs and drugs, with a hydrophilic core inside. The drug-loading efficacy of Drug@Copo_FAs varied from 30 to 84% depending on the properties of the loaded drug. Drug@Copo_FAs could convert the surface charge from anions to cations in an acidic environment, such as a tumor microenvironment with a pH 6.4, leading to increased cellular uptake compared to that observed with liposome. Drug@Copo_FAs minimized the initial release of encapsulated hydrophilic drugs to <10–20% over 24 h, minimizing the loss of encapsulated drugs in the blood circulation. Drug@Copo_FAs themselves emit a fluorescent signal, enabling in vivo tracking and demonstrating increased tumor-selective accumulation potency compared to liposomes. When applied for the treatment of pancreatic cancer, Drug@Copo_FAs showed enhanced tumor growth inhibition efficacy compared to Gem@Lipo, a general drug delivery carrier. Thus, Coposomes can be considered novel drug delivery platforms that can be fabricated via conjugation with hydrophilic PCDs and hydrophobic fatty chains. They demonstrated the potential for use in the treatment of various diseases.

Hongjae Kim: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Kyoung Sub Kim: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.
